title: Chapter 13
text: Chapter Ten T he afternoon dragged for Lara. She was accustomed to labor from dawn to dusk on most days; this idleness was torture . She took another hot shower, scrubbing her hair, body, and teeth. Then she stood — despite Ronin’s talk about not being wasteful — in the falling water for at least fifteen minutes, letting it melt away her aches . After stepping out of the tub and drying off, she dressed in her own clothes. Their smell alone was a comfort, reminding her of her old life; it hadn’t been good, but it was familiar, and that grounded her. She didn’t know if she’d ever feel like she belonged in Ronin’s world . Returning to her room, she placed the shirt Ronin gave her in the dresser’s top drawer, wondering if he’d been joking about people filling these things up with clothing. Why would someone need so many clothes ? She explored the other upstairs rooms. They were similar to hers in appearance —bare of furniture apart from beds and dressers, no decorations to be seen. The room closest to hers had pale blue walls instead of the white that covered the rest of the house. Still, the little differences, like the way her bedding was crumpled and disturbed and the collection of trinkets atop the dresser, made hers feel inviting . She stopped in the doorway of Ronin’s room, staring inside, oddly hesitant to enter. If he didn’t want her to go in, he’d have closed the door. It wasn’t like he cared about privacy, right ? Stepping across the threshold, she swept her gaze over the room. His bed was perfectly made, with squared corners and not so much as a wrinkle on the blanket. That made sense; Ronin didn’t sleep . What did he do to keep himself occupied? Did he just stare blankly at the walls? Did he think of … No. Not going there right now . After the space he’d given her last night, after his apology, it was wrong coming in without his permission … But what could he possibly have that he didn’t want her to see, anyway? It wasn’t like bots kept things for sentimental value. Hell, most humans didn’t even do that. Lara was an exception. People only cared about things relating to survival — food and drink, warmth and shelter. Though the specific needs were different, bots operated the same way . A large chest sat at the foot of the bed, with several tools, brushes, and narrow rods laid atop it. Amidst these items was the gun he’d held when he burst into the toilet room last night, having rushed to protect Lara after hearing her scream . Lara stared at the dull black gun. Her heart thumped. If she’d had one that night … She stepped forward and reached for it, stopping suddenly just before her fingers touched the metal. Ronin hadn’t been stopped by three shots to his abdomen; a single cut could be the end for a human. This little gun wouldn’t do any damage to Ronin . Why did he own it? Why had he left it behind ? A test, perhaps. But if she fired it at him — assuming she even hit her target — it wouldn’t do enough harm for her to escape retaliation . She swallowed her temptation and dropped her hand, moving on to the double doors on the opposite wall. They slid open on hidden rails. It was a little room, even smaller than her shack, with clothing hung on either side. Ronin’s clothing. Leaning in, she took a deep breath, realizing what she’d done only after Ronin’s earth-and-steel scent filled her nostrils . Wanderer. Dustwalker. How much time had he spent out there ? Why was she thinking about him so much ? Annoyed with herself, she returned to her room and closed the door behind her. Without meaning to, she compared it to Ronin’s . She made the bed, keeping the memory of his at the front of her mind. No matter how many ways she tried, no matter how careful she was, the blanket always hung off one side more than the other, always had unsightly lumps and wrinkles in it . It was going to get messed up when she went to sleep. Why bother ? With a huff, she sat on the edge of the bed, drumming her fingers atop the soft blanket. Her gaze wandered, eventually stopping on the window. Afternoon light filtered through the coverings, filling the room with warmth, and the shadows of the trees danced on the bottom panes . There were three rows of windows outside, she realized. This was only the second floor. She’d seen a third last night, staring up at Ronin’s dark, looming home. It had to be directly above her room . So, where was the staircase up to the next level? That seemed a large thing to miss, having been through all the rooms. There was another sliding door on one of her walls, leading to a recess barely large enough for a child to lie in; a smaller version of the room Ronin kept his clothes in . “Enough with the damned clothes, already,” she muttered . Her search of the room was quick, but thorough, ending when she wrestled the heavy dresser forward to reveal the solid wall behind it . Lara entered the hallway, brow furrowed, and swept her eyes back and forth as she considered the mystery. She paced along its length, stumped. Maybe the upper window was just for show? Nothing about these buildings made sense to her as it was; it wouldn’t surprise her if whole parts of them served no practical purpose . Frustrated and disappointed, she tilted her head back and sighed. Her eyes widened at the frayed string dangling from the ceiling. There was a rectangle cut in the material around it, as though it were an oddly-placed door. It was bordered by the same strips of wood that edged the meeting place of the walls and floor . Standing on the balls of her feet, she reached for the string. It was too high. She jumped, brushing the end and setting it to sway wildly. On her second attempt, she caught a solid hold of the string, using her weight to yank down . Dust showered her, and pain exploded in her temple as a ladder slid down from overhead and struck her . “Fuck!” Teeth clenched, she squeezed her eyes shut and pressed a hand over the throbbing point of impact. Tears welled in her eyes. She placed her other hand on the ladder to steady herself . “Should have a warning posted or something. Like a picture, making the danger clear — possible beheading when opened .” Lara opened her eyes and looked up at the opening in the ceiling. Motes of dust floated lazily through the air all around it. And, to her annoyance, she noticed a little sign on the side of the ladder. Black writing and images of little stick people — or were they bots? — getting hit by the ladder . “Yeah, that’s the perfect place for it .” The ache in her head continued as she climbed the ladder. Its old springs squeaked in protest of her weight . When her head emerged in the new space, she stopped. The air here was heavier and warmer than that in the hallway below. A thick layer of dust coated everything, all cast in the afternoon sunlight streaming through the rectangular window ahead. The ceiling was slanted, extremely low to either side and meeting in a high peak at the center . Lara climbed into the room, turning on hands and knees to look through the opening. The floor below was covered in clumps of dust. Without her weight on it, the ladder lifted slightly; she pressed down on it to keep it extended. Whatever mechanism had locked it open creaked, and the whole thing swung back up, ladder folding into place on its own . She stared at the sealed entry for a few seconds before she stood and dusted her hands off. She’d deal with that problem soon enough. Turning, she studied the room. The slanted walls — from which protruded the tips of countless nails — were lined with wooden beams running from one end to another. The floor was also wood, constructed with close-fitting boards that lacked the impossible polish of the ones downstairs . The room looked…old. Unlike the rest of the house, there was no gleam of cleanliness up here, no coat of paint to hide the age. The musty smell of rotted wood filled the air. This room had been neglected for a long, long time . Stacks of gray boxes stood halfway between the hatch and the window. Nearby was a small, square table with books piled atop it. The chair in front of the table matched the one in the main room downstairs . Lara walked to the table and picked up one of the books, brushing the thick layer of dust off the cover. Between the discoloration and the cracks in the material, she couldn’t tell what it had depicted. The pages inside were stained yellow, crinkly and brittle, but they were intact. She gently ran a finger along the first line of words. Paper was a rare thing amongst humans, complete books even more so . Setting it aside, she leaned down to look at another book. This one had a larger, thinner cover, which depicted a car in full repair. Glancing through the pages, she saw dozens more cars, in more colors and shapes than she’d thought possible. The closest she’d seen were the rusted-out remains of a few old cars some people had used to build their dwellings back home . Her attention shifted to the case of shelves beside the window. It was like the one in her shack, though this was larger and filled with jars instead of trinkets. Moving to it, she lifted one of the jars, brushing away dust and cobwebs. She wiped her hand clean on her skirt . “And I just washed it, too,” she muttered . The jar was heavy, and something dark sloshed inside, but the dirt caked on the glass was too thick to see through. Gripping the lid, she twisted her hand. The metal ring resisted for a moment before the rust gave way. The metal plate beneath proved more difficult. She pressed her fingers on its edge, wincing as she pried at it. Finally, it released its suction, coming loose with a pop . The foulest odor she’d ever smelled hit Lara directly in the face. Gagging, she nearly dropped the jar, extending her arm to keep it as far away as possible. She glanced at the unidentifiable black sludge inside through slitted, tear- filled eyes . “Ugh!” Slapping the seal back on top, she quickly screwed the ring back on and returned the jar to its place. “What was that ?” She reached up to wipe her face, but caught another whiff of the smell on her fingers. Nose crinkling, she rubbed her hands roughly on her skirt . “Yeah. Gonna need to wash these again .” Strange how her attitude had changed overnight; she’d rarely washed her clothes, before. It only made them wear out faster, and she knew they’d be filthy the next day anyway, after hours spent in heat and dirt. But feeling soft cloth against clean skin…it was going to be difficult to let that go. She’d have to enjoy it while she could . It was only a matter of time before Ronin lost interest in her . The room, despite the thick dust, was comfortable. Though it was far from perfect, it wasn’t falling apart; this place had been lived in. It reminded her of her little shack. Long enough up here, and she might forget she was in the Bot District at all . She used the end of her skirt to wipe away the grime on the window. The grassy field stretched out below, and from this height the pond near its center was visible. Was this how humans used to live? In quiet comfort ? As Lara turned, her foot bumped into something. She glanced down at a pile of tattered blankets. Crouching, she lifted them up; the top blanket crumbled to fibers at her touch. She tossed the bunch aside, revealing an assortment of items. The clothes were in the same ratty condition as the blankets, and looked to have belonged to a man. They were wrapped around a few more books . But it was the strange device with the wire trailing off it that caught her attention first. Rectangular, it was small enough to fit comfortably in her hand, and had a few buttons on the front and sides. Its glass face was cracked. The wire split after about two feet, ending in a pair of small, strangely- shaped buds . Placing it atop the blankets, she looked through the books. The first few were thin and flimsy, like the one with the car pictures, and she nearly dropped one when she opened it . “Oh.” She’d turned to a page with a large photo of a naked woman, hands lifting her oversized breasts. Many of the other pages displayed similar images — women in various poses, either naked or damned near it . They reminded her of the synths at Kitty’s. Their bodies were full and lush, their skin flawless, without a hair out of place — and most without hair on their pussies. Some of them had unnaturally large breasts, bigger than any Lara had seen. There was color on their lips and around their eyes — deep reds and pinks, shades of blue, green, purple, and black. With their perfect figures and faces, she would have thought they were bots, but no . They all had belly buttons. Crazy as it seemed, they were human . She set it aside . The book at the bottom of the stack was different from the others. It wasn’t as wide, but it was thicker, the outside covered in old leather. She opened it and carefully looked through the pages . This one had been written by hand. Somehow, that made it more special. She’d never met anyone who could read, much less write, so it seemed an impossible skill. Though she couldn’t understand the words, for a moment she felt closer to the past than ever before. In the center, she found a few loose photos. The same people were present through all of them — a man, a woman, and two children. Had they lived in this house? Hidden in this room ? She glanced at the blankets and clothing, the single chair, the jars on the shelf. It had likely only been one person here . Replacing the pictures, she went to the table and gently laid the book atop it. Her eyes fell on the stacked boxes. What other little treasures were here to discover ? * * * R onin entered his dwelling, closing and locking the door behind him. His fingertips lingered on the knob. It was cold and smooth; texture and accurate temperature readings added dimensions to touch that he’d forgotten about during his long time in the Dust . The sun sank toward the western horizon, streaming orange light through the front windows which cast long shadows over his worktable. He’d hoped Lara would be in the main room when he returned, sitting at the table or perched on one of the wide window sills, but he doubted she could sit still for very long under most circumstances . Crossing the room, he placed his pack on the table and pulled off his coat, running his fingers over the rough fabric before tossing it over the back of the chair. The needle and thread he’d used to repair her boot were still out; he’d have to put them away later . He stepped into the kitchen. She wasn’t there, either, and why would she be? There wasn’t any food for her yet. He grasped the refrigerator by the sides, pulled it away from the wall, and slipped behind it to plug the cord into the electrical outlet . Something inside it rattled for several seconds before the sound evened to a soft, steady hum. He was, for once, grateful for the maintenance bots keeping the home appliances in Cheyenne in working order. Lara’s food would last longer in the refrigerator, which meant fewer trips to the vendor for Ronin . He pressed his hand to the refrigerator’s flat face, the sensors in his skin detecting the minute grooves in the brushed metal, and pushed it back into place. He’d store the newly-obtained food inside soon enough. Lara was likely hungry after his five-hour absence, and she’d appreciate choosing what food to eat first . Moving to the foot of the stairs, he called her name . Seconds ticked by. After a full minute had passed, he went up. She was likely resting. Ronin understood sleep only in that it was a necessary biological function for humans, much like her having to go . He could never truly experience it, so he didn’t truly comprehend, but he would still accommodate her needs . All the upstairs doors were open. Though there wasn’t much to see, she’d been familiarizing herself with her surroundings. Many of the home’s furnishings had been removed before Ronin took up residence here, and even the few remaining pieces were more than he required . He went to her room, finding the bed made — inexpertly — and her trinkets spread atop the dresser. Otherwise, there was no sign of her . The deadbolt had been locked upon his return, and he’d detected no errant air currents indicating an open window. Could she have been taken? Or had she found a way out on her own ? He focused his optics on her collection, and, after moving further into her room, discovered her boots and bag on the floor beside the dresser. No, she hadn’t left on her own. She’d never leave these things behind . She wasn’t in the other bedrooms, and his gear — including the pistol — was still where he left it, atop the chest at the foot of his bed. More evidence that she hadn’t departed on her own. All these items would aid in her survival; to leave behind her own belongings and his openly displayed tools was madness . His processors whirred as he returned to the hallway and paced from one end to the other, running hundreds, thousands of simulations at once. None of them could fully account for the single greatest variable — human unpredictability . Taken, then? He was certain Warlord kept keys to every residence in the Bot District, and it would only require a single gearhead to subdue Lara. What history did she have with Cheyenne’s ruler ? Ronin halted abruptly, tilting his head down. Thick clumps of dust lay on the carpet, some already trampled into the fibers by his boots. His optics swept up to the attic hatch . He’d hadn’t gone up there once since taking up residence. Why would she ? Reaching up, he grasped the broken pull-string and tugged the hatch open . The whine of old springs and hinges warned him. He raised his free hand and caught the ladder before it connected with his head. Above, someone gasped . Floorboards creaked overhead, and Lara peered over the edge, with cobwebs clinging to her messy hair and a smudge of dirt on one cheek. Briefly, he shut out everything but his sensory input. Lara was here . Safe . And she was beautiful . “Did you know somebody lived up here ?” Her question was so unexpected that he wasn’t sure how to process it. Hello or what’s going on would have been easier to respond to . “What are you talking about?” He lowered the ladder. The wood groaned as he mounted it, boards flexing enough beneath his weight that he had to adjust his stabilizers . Lara backed away, allowing him enough space to climb onto the dusty attic floor. Her footprints were everywhere, in a meandering trail from one side of the attic to the other, and back again. It was eighteen degrees warmer up here than in the hallway below. Her damp hair was plastered to her face, and a sheen of perspiration covered her skin . “You’re hurt,” he said, raising a hand to brush her hair away from the dried blood at her temple . “It’s nothing.” She ducked out of his reach and pulled more hair over the wound. “The ladder had the upper hand on me.” One corner of her mouth lifted, and she snickered. “ Get it ?” Ronin dropped his hand to his side. Internally, he combed his stored data for information about human head injuries. Were nonsensical ramblings and statements without context sign of deeper trauma ? “I…got everything I intended at the Market ?” “Wow. Okay. We’ll add no appreciation for witty humor to the list. Anyway—wait. What happened to your hands ?” She moved around to his side, staring at his hands. He lifted them, turning his palms toward the ceiling, and inspected the new skin. It was strange to see them this way again, after so long. They were undoubtedly his hands, but seemed somehow out of place . “Went to the Clinic and had them reskinned. Been a long time since I’ve done it. I figured it was overdue .” “You can do that? Like, grow new skin ?” Did she realize how close to him she was moving in her curiosity ? “It’s synthesized from various materials. Not grown .” She looked into his optics, her brows furrowed. “Why don’t the gearheads do it , then ?” “Because they don’t want to be mistaken for humans .” “Yeah. Because we’re so horrible.” She rolled her eyes and walked toward the window. “Come look at this .” Not sure how to respond to her comment, he followed her to the far end of the attic. He took in the scene — the bookcase full of jars, the blankets and clothing on the floor, the table and chair — as Lara knelt . She picked up a book with a brown leather cover. “I can’t read it, but someone wrote this.” She held it out to him . “Every book was written by someone,” Ronin replied. He took the book, running his fingertips over the textured surface . “No, smart ass. This one was written by hand . Not like the other ones. And this,” she gestured to the blankets, “was where he slept .” His optics followed her gesture before returning to the book. Carefully, he opened it. The pages were stiff, but in surprisingly good condition . Those things killed people today. Marched into town and just started killing people. I watched from the window as people were dragged into the park and executed . “And this is what he ate,” Lara said, calling Ronin’s attention back to her. She stood near the shelf, holding a jar with a dark, unidentifiable substance inside it. “Trust me when I say not to open them .” Ronin studied the objects one at a time, cataloguing them in his memory. It was impossible to say how long ago the words had been written, how long ago the writer had lived. Many, many years, certainly . “ He ?” Lara stepped close to him, turning the pages to reveal the photographs tucked in the middle of the book. The top one was a picture of a family, the adult male and female both with dark hair and eyes. The two children resembled them closely . “Pretty sure it was him,” she said, tapping the man’s face . Humans didn’t look like that anymore. It wasn’t that the people in the picture had drastically different features than modern people; the basic human facial structure and typical proportions were unchanged. No, it was the light in their eyes, the genuine smiles on their faces . The people in the photo were happy, healthy, alive . “And he had a great view,” Lara said from the window. Outside, the sunlight sparkled on the surface of the pond and cast golden halos around the treetops . I watched from the window as people were dragged into the park and executed . “Come away from there,” Ronin said, tone sharp even to his own receptors . “Why?” she asked, eyes narrowed on him . “There’s food downstairs, and you’re likely in need of hydration after spending so much time up here. Go clean yourself up and have something to eat .” An angry crease had formed between her eyebrows, but it vanished once she looked down at herself. Dust clung to her clothing and sweat- dampened skin . “Yeah…guess I didn’t notice. I maybe got a little carried away.” She plucked restlessly at the hem of her skirt before letting it drop . “I found some more clothes for you at the Market,” Ronin said. “They might not fit quite right, but they’ll be better than nothing until we can adjust them.” His boots thumped on the floorboards as he walked to the hatch. “Go on. I’d rather you get down safe before it collapses under me .” “Okay, okay.” With one last glance at the window, she hurried over and climbed down . Ronin’s optics lingered on the scene for a few more seconds before he shifted them to the book in his hand. JOURNAL was barely legible on the front cover. He slipped it into his pocket and headed downstairs .
